claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children that I'd like to claim Child Benefit for. Their names are Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine eligibility for Alex and Blake.

To start, **how old are Alex and Blake?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Since Alex is 17 and Blake is 18, I need to check if they're still in approved education or training, as this affects eligibility for children over 16.

**Are Alex and Blake currently in approved education or training? If yes, please let me know what type of education or training each of them is in.**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is doing A-levels at sixth form college. Blake has actually left education - he's not studying anymore.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to ask about Blake specifically, since he's 18 and has left education.

**Has Blake started paid work for 24 hours or more a week, or is he receiving any benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't working that much - he just does a bit here and there, nothing full-time. And no, he's not getting any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Now I need to understand your living situation and relationship with Alex and Blake.

**Do both Alex and Blake currently live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both of them live with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just one more question to complete my assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment:

## Eligibility Decision

**For Alex (age 17): ✓ ELIGIBLE**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex is 17 years old and doing A-levels at sixth form college, which counts as approved education
- Child Benefit extends to children under 20 who remain in approved education or training
- Alex lives with you in the UK
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

**For Blake (age 18): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE**
You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake is 18 and has left education
- For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit only continues if they stay in approved education or training
- Since Blake has left education and is not in approved training, eligibility has ended
- The fact that Blake isn't working full-time or claiming benefits doesn't restore eligibility once approved education has ended

## What This Means
You can claim or continue to receive Child Benefit for Alex while he continues his A-levels. For Blake, Child Benefit would have stopped when he left education. If you've been receiving Child Benefit for Blake since he left education, you should inform HMRC about this change in circumstances.

Do you have any questions about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is currently doing A-levels at sixth form college, which counts as approved education. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16, but this extends to children under 20 if they stay in approved education or training. Alex lives with the claimant in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 18 years old and has left education. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can continue to claim Child Benefit for children aged 16-19 only if they stay in approved education or training. Since Blake has left education and is not in approved training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. The fact that Blake is not working full-time (24+ hours) or claiming benefits does not restore eligibility once approved education or training has ended.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (17) and Blake (18). Both live with the claimant in Manchester, UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child. Alex remains eligible because he is under 20 and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form). Blake is not eligible because he is over 16, has left education, and is not in approved training - the eligibility for 16-19 year olds requires them to remain in approved education or training.

Since Blake has left education and is not in approved training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. The fact that Blake is not working full-time (24+ hours) or claiming benefits does not restore eligibility once approved education or training has ended.

Alex remains eligible because he is under 20 and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form). Blake is not eligible because he is over 16, has left education, and is not in approved training - the eligibility for 16-19 year olds requires them to remain in approved education or training.
